events.title: Peña Nieto Inaugurated as 57th President
events.detail: Enrique Peña Nieto inaugurated at the Chamber of Deputies, Mexico City, returning the PRI to power after a 12-year absence. Violent clashes outside between protesters and Federal Police left 100+ detained. The ceremony marked the end of two consecutive PAN administrations (Fox and Calderón).

events.title: Pacto por México Signed by Three Major Parties
events.detail: Peña Nieto, PAN president Gustavo Madero, and PRD president Jesús Zambrano signed the Pacto por México at the Palacio Nacional — a 95-commitment legislative pact to cooperate on economic, social, and democratic reforms. The unprecedented tripartite agreement was designed to break congressional gridlock and deliver structural changes.

events.title: Key Cabinet Appointments: Videgaray and Osorio Chong
events.detail: Luis Videgaray Caso sworn in as Finance Secretary (SHCP) and Miguel Ángel Osorio Chong as Interior Secretary (SEGOB). Videgaray became Peña Nieto's most influential advisor and architect of structural reforms; Osorio Chong managed the national security dossier throughout the sexenio.

events.title: Education Reform: Teacher Evaluation System Approved
events.detail: Constitutional amendment to Article 3 created the Servicio Profesional Docente — a nationwide teacher evaluation system — and stripped the SNTE teachers' union of control over hiring and promotions. The reform drew fierce opposition from the radical CNTE faction, particularly in Oaxaca, Guerrero, and Chiapas, who viewed it as an attack on labor rights.

events.title: Telecommunications Reform: Breaking the Duopoly
events.detail: Constitutional amendment and secondary laws overhauled Mexico's telecom sector, creating the IFT (Instituto Federal de Telecomunicaciones) as an autonomous regulator. Targeted the Televisa/Telmex duopoly, ordering Telmex to open its network and designating dominant players subject to regulation. Considered the most ambitious telecom reform in Latin America at the time.

events.title: Energy Reform Proposed: Opening Oil Sector to Private Capital
events.detail: Peña Nieto presented a constitutional amendment to Articles 25, 27, and 28 before Congress, proposing to allow private and foreign investment in oil, gas, and electricity for the first time since the 1938 nationalization. The move ended the 75-year Pemex/CFE monopoly and was the most politically controversial reform of the sexenio, drawing protests from AMLO's movement and leftist parties.

events.title: PRD Withdraws from Pacto por México
events.detail: The center-left PRD withdrew from the Pacto por México in protest against the proposed energy reform, which it characterized as privatization of the national patrimony. The collapse of the tripartite pact as an operational instrument left PRI and PAN to push energy reform through Congress, exposing the limits of the cross-party consensus.

events.title: Energy Reform Constitutional Amendment and Secondary Laws Passed
events.detail: The Chamber of Deputies approved the energy reform constitutional amendment (354 for, 134 against) in November; secondary laws published in the Diario Oficial on December 20, 2013. Created the CNH (National Hydrocarbons Commission) and CRE (Energy Regulatory Commission). Pemex and CFE converted to 'Empresas Productivas del Estado' — retaining state ownership but operating commercially.

events.title: Financial Reform: 34 Laws Amended to Increase Bank Competition
events.detail: Financial Reform amended 34 laws to increase bank credit, lower interest costs, strengthen insolvency law, and empower development banks (BANOBRAS, NAFIN). Allowed banks to recover collateral more easily (juicio oral mercantil). Aimed at Mexico's chronically low domestic credit as a share of GDP — among the lowest in Latin America.

events.title: Fiscal Reform Takes Effect: VAT Increase and New Taxes
events.detail: The fiscal reform homogenized the VAT at 16% (eliminating the 11% border rate), introduced new taxes on sugary drinks and junk food (IEPS), and added a capital gains tax. Critics called it regressive; the government argued it broadened the tax base without raising the income tax rate. The reform generated controversy along the northern border where costs rose sharply.

events.title: El Chapo Guzmán Captured in Mazatlán
events.detail: Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán Loera, head of the Sinaloa Cartel and Mexico's most wanted criminal, was arrested without a shot fired by SEMAR marines at the Miramar condominium in Mazatlán, Sinaloa, assisted by the U.S. DEA. It was his first capture since escaping from Puente Grande prison in 2001. The arrest was the most celebrated security success of the Peña Nieto administration.

events.title: Political-Electoral Reform: INE Created, Re-election Permitted
events.detail: Comprehensive political reform created the INE (Instituto Nacional Electoral) to replace the IFE, allowed independent candidates for the first time, permitted legislative re-election beginning 2018, and introduced a recall referendum mechanism. The reform was backed by all parties as a modernization of Mexico's electoral architecture.

events.title: Ronda Uno Launched: First Oil Block Bidding Under Energy Reform
events.detail: Mexico's first competitive oil block bidding round under the new energy reform framework was launched, offering 169 oil contracts to private and international companies. The first results were disappointing — only 2 of 14 shallow-water blocks awarded in Round 1.1 (Jul 2015) — but subsequent rounds attracted major international oil companies (Shell, Total, ENI, Chevron).

events.title: 43 Normalistas Forcibly Disappeared in Iguala, Guerrero
events.detail: Forty-three male students from the Escuela Normal Rural 'Raúl Isidro Burgos' (Ayotzinapa) were forcibly disappeared in Iguala, Guerrero. Students had commandeered buses to travel to Mexico City for October 2 commemorations. Iguala municipal police, reportedly acting on orders of Mayor José Luis Abarca (allegedly linked to the Guerreros Unidos cartel), intercepted and handed over the students. Six people were killed in attacks on the buses the same night; 25+ were wounded.

events.title: Guerrero Governor Aguirre Rivero Resigns
events.detail: Under intense public pressure over the Ayotzinapa disappearances, Guerrero Governor Ángel Aguirre Rivero (PRD) resigned. Federal forces took control of Iguala and the state's security operations. The resignation reflected the scale of institutional failure in Guerrero exposed by the case.

events.title: Iguala Mayor José Luis Abarca Arrested in Mexico City
events.detail: Iguala Mayor José Luis Abarca and his wife María de los Ángeles Pineda Villa — alleged Guerreros Unidos cartel associates — were arrested after being found hiding in Mexico City. Abarca was charged with ordering the municipal police to intercept and hand over the normalista students. The arrests provided the government with its main identified perpetrators.

events.title: Casa Blanca Scandal: Aristegui Exposes Rivera's Mansion from Higa
events.detail: Carmen Aristegui and her MVS Noticias investigative team published 'La Casa Blanca de Enrique Peña Nieto' — a 7-minute video exposé revealing that first lady Angélica Rivera owned a MXN $54 million mansion at Paseo de la Reforma 150 (Lomas de Chapultepec), built and financed by Grupo Higa, a construction firm that had received billions in government contracts under Peña Nieto as governor of Estado de México and as president. Finance Secretary Videgaray was simultaneously revealed to own a home purchased from the same contractor.

events.title: AG Murillo Karam Presents 'Verdad Histórica' on Ayotzinapa
events.detail: Attorney General Jesús Murillo Karam presented the government's official account of the disappearances: that Guerreros Unidos cartel killed the students, incinerated their bodies for 15 hours at the Cocula municipal dump, and placed remains in plastic bags dumped in the San Juan River. One bone fragment from Alexander Mora Venancio was identified via Argentine forensic experts. The press conference ended with Murillo Karam's notorious 'Ya me cansé' (I'm tired) remark, triggering widespread outrage.

events.title: Carmen Aristegui Fired from MVS Radio Amid Government Pressure
events.detail: MVS Radio fired investigative journalist Carmen Aristegui, reportedly under pressure from the Peña Nieto government following the Casa Blanca investigation. The Committee to Protect Journalists (CPJ) and Reporters Without Borders condemned the firing. Aristegui subsequently launched Aristegui Noticias as an independent digital outlet that became one of Mexico's most prominent investigative platforms.

events.title: GIEI Report 1: 'Verdad Histórica' Is Scientifically Impossible
events.detail: The IACHR's independent expert group (GIEI) released its first report, concluding that the Cocula dump incineration hypothesis was 'scientifically impossible': atmospheric models, soil samples, and satellite imagery showed no evidence of a large-scale fire capable of destroying 43 bodies on the night in question. GIEI also documented a fifth bus (Estrella Roja) likely carrying a heroin shipment and identified the 27th Infantry Battalion as present in Iguala without intervening.

events.title: GIEI Report 2: Military Involvement; Mexico Declines to Extend Mandate
events.detail: The GIEI released its second report, deepening findings about the role of the 27th Infantry Battalion, identifying additional witnesses intimidated by authorities, and accusing PGR's AIC (under Tomás Zerón) of evidence tampering and witness torture. Mexico declined to extend the GIEI mandate, prompting the group to depart. Their conclusions fundamentally undermined the official account and implicated state actors in the disappearances.

events.title: Midterm Elections: Morena Emerges, PRI Weakened
events.detail: Midterm federal elections saw the PRI retain a plurality but with a significantly reduced margin. AMLO's new Morena party (founded October 2014) won Mexico City Federal District delegate races and established itself as a genuine third force in Mexican politics. The elections demonstrated growing discontent with Peña Nieto's government amid the Ayotzinapa crisis and Casa Blanca scandal.

events.title: El Chapo Escapes Altiplano Prison Through 1.5 km Tunnel
events.detail: Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán escaped from the Altiplano maximum-security federal prison (CEFERESO No. 1) in Almoloya de Juárez through a 1.5-kilometer tunnel equipped with lighting, ventilation, and a motorcycle rail system. The tunnel opened inside the shower stall of his cell. The escape was a catastrophic political scandal for the Peña Nieto government; Interior Secretary Osorio Chong survived calls to resign. A $100 million reward was issued for Chapo's recapture.

events.title: El Chapo Recaptured in Los Mochis, Sinaloa
events.detail: After six months as a fugitive, El Chapo was recaptured by SEMAR marines in a pre-dawn raid on a safe house in Los Mochis, Sinaloa. Chapo briefly escaped through the sewer system but was captured on a road outside the city. The recapture coincided with publication of Sean Penn's Rolling Stone interview with Chapo; authorities said the Penn/Kate del Castillo meeting had helped locate the fugitive. Questions arose about whether the recapture was staged to enable a politically timed extradition.

events.title: Nochixtlán Massacre: 8 Protesters Killed by Federal Police
events.detail: Federal Police clashed with CNTE teacher protesters blocking a highway near Nochixtlán, Oaxaca. Eight people were killed and more than 100 injured in the confrontation. The incident became known as the 'Nochixtlán massacre' and was widely condemned by human rights organizations as a disproportionate use of force against civilian protesters. CNDH issued a recommendation citing excessive force.

events.title: Peña Nieto Hosts Donald Trump at Los Pinos
events.detail: In a surprise announcement, Peña Nieto invited then-candidate Donald Trump — who had called Mexican immigrants rapists — for a meeting at Los Pinos. Trump used the opportunity to appear presidential; Peña Nieto failed to publicly confront Trump about his promised border wall. The meeting was widely criticized domestically as humiliating for Mexico. Finance Secretary Videgaray, the architect of the visit, briefly lost his post before returning as Foreign Minister.

events.title: Trump Election Shock: Peso Crashes to 21.38/USD
events.detail: Donald Trump's unexpected victory in the U.S. presidential election sent the Mexican peso to a record low of 21.38 per USD, a 13% single-day drop. Mexico's economy faced profound uncertainty over Trump's threats to renegotiate or exit NAFTA, impose tariffs, and build a border wall. The currency shock triggered an inflationary spiral that contributed to the gasolinazo of January 2017.

events.title: Governor Javier Duarte Flees to Guatemala Amid Corruption Charges
events.detail: Veracruz PRI Governor Javier Duarte fled to Guatemala facing charges of embezzling billions from state health and education funds through ghost companies and fraudulent federal transfers. His case exposed systematic corruption within PRI governorships. He was captured in Guatemala in April 2017, extradited to Mexico, and sentenced to 9 years in prison in 2018.

events.title: Gasolinazo: Fuel Prices Jump 14–20% Overnight
events.detail: As part of the energy sector liberalization, the government removed fuel price subsidies on January 1, 2017, causing gasoline prices to jump 14–20% overnight. The policy triggered the largest popular backlash of the sexenio: nationwide protests, looting of more than 1,500 businesses, highway blockades, and at least 6 deaths in unrest. The term 'gasolinazo' — a Mexican slang term for gas price shocks — became a symbol of government insensitivity to the poor.

events.title: El Chapo Extradited to the United States
events.detail: El Chapo was extradited from Ciudad Juárez to New York via DEA helicopter and aircraft, following Mexico's Supreme Court approval of the extradition in November 2016. He faced trial in the Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn) for drug trafficking, murder conspiracy, and money laundering charges. His extradition removed a major political liability from Mexico but also removed him from Mexican judicial accountability.

events.title: Odebrecht Scandal: $10.5M Paid to PRI 2012 Campaign via Pemex CEO
events.detail: Leaked Finance Ministry documents and subsequent FGR investigation confirmed that Brazilian construction giant Odebrecht paid approximately $10.5 million in bribes via Pemex CEO Emilio Lozoya Austin to fund the PRI's 2012 presidential campaign. Lozoya testified in 2020 that senators also received bribes to vote for the energy reform. The scandal implicated Peña Nieto directly in criminal proceedings launched under the AMLO administration.

sources.name: Mexicanos Contra la Corrupción y la Impunidad (MCCI)

events.title: M8.2 Earthquake Strikes Chiapas-Oaxaca Coast
events.detail: The strongest earthquake in Mexico in 100 years (Mw 8.2) struck off the coast at the Chiapas-Oaxaca border (Tehuantepec region) at 11:49 PM local time. The earthquake killed 98 people — 72 in Oaxaca, 15 in Chiapas, others in Tabasco — and caused massive damage in Juchitán de Zaragoza, Oaxaca, where the historic town center was effectively destroyed. More than 800 aftershocks followed.

events.title: M7.1 Mexico City Earthquake: 369 Dead, Rébsamen School Collapses
events.detail: A Mw 7.1 earthquake struck the Puebla-Morelos border at 1:14 PM local time on the 32nd anniversary of the 1985 Mexico City earthquake. 369 people were killed: 228 in Mexico City, 74 in Morelos, 45 in Puebla, and 13 in Estado de México. Enrique Rébsamen primary school in Tlalpan collapsed, killing 26 children and 6 adults. Massive civil society mobilization — thousands of volunteer rescuers ('topos') — contrasted with government criticism for slow and bureaucratic response.

events.title: Interior Security Law Formalizes Military in Domestic Security
events.detail: The Ley de Seguridad Interior was approved by Congress over fierce civil society and IACHR opposition. The law formalized military deployments in domestic security operations for up to four years (renewable), giving constitutional backing to the existing practice of using the armed forces for policing. Human rights groups called it a legal framework for military impunity. AMLO vowed to repeal it, which he did in 2019 by replacing it with the National Guard framework.

events.title: Estafa Maestra: MXN $7.67B Diverted Through Phantom Companies
events.detail: Mexicanos Contra la Corrupción (MCCI) and Animal Político published the 'Estafa Maestra' investigation, documenting how approximately MXN $7.67 billion (≈ USD $400 million) was diverted from federal agencies — including SEDESOL and SEDATU under Rosario Robles — through 11 public universities to ghost companies, enriching senior federal officials. The scheme involved falsified contracts and companies with no real employees. Rosario Robles was arrested in 2019 and convicted in 2021.

events.title: Plagiarism Scandal: 29% of Peña Nieto's Law Thesis Unattributed
events.detail: MCCI (Mexicanos Contra la Corrupción) published an investigation showing that approximately 29% of Peña Nieto's 1991 law thesis at Universidad Panamericana was copied from other sources without attribution. The university conducted an internal review but declined to revoke his degree. The case was widely seen as confirming the broader culture of impunity shielding elite Mexicans from academic, legal, and political accountability.

events.title: AMLO Wins Landslide: 53.2% — Largest Margin Since 1994
events.detail: Andrés Manuel López Obrador (Morena) won the presidential election with 53.2% of the vote — a record 31-percentage-point margin over second-place Ricardo Anaya (PAN coalition, 22.3%). PRI candidate José Antonio Meade received only 16%. Morena also won a supermajority in Congress and six governorships. The result was universally interpreted as a rejection of Peña Nieto's corruption, impunity, Ayotzinapa handling, and economic stagnation. It was the largest PRI electoral defeat in the party's history.

events.title: Citizen Consultation Votes 70% to Cancel NAIM Airport
events.detail: AMLO's citizen consultation voted 70% to cancel the New Mexico City International Airport (NAIM) in Texcoco — a $13 billion project that was already 30%+ complete with bonds sold in international capital markets. The Peña Nieto government (still in office until November 30) expressed concern about contract obligations. The cancellation became a major controversy during the transition, with investor confidence shaken.

events.title: Peña Nieto Transfers Presidency to AMLO at Palacio Nacional
events.detail: Enrique Peña Nieto formally transferred the presidential sash to Andrés Manuel López Obrador at the Palacio Nacional, ending his six-year term. Peña Nieto delivered a final address defending structural reforms and citing poverty reduction. He subsequently moved to Spain. By 2021, he was under investigation — per Emilio Lozoya's testimony — for personally receiving Odebrecht bribes. USMCA (successor to NAFTA) had been signed on the same day, a final achievement of his administration.

events.title: USMCA Signed: NAFTA Successor Secured Before End of Sexenio
events.detail: The United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA), negotiated by Economy Secretary Ildefonso Guajardo under the leadership of Finance-turned-Foreign Minister Luis Videgaray, was signed in Buenos Aires on November 30, 2018 — the day of the presidential transfer. The agreement modernized the 1994 NAFTA with new provisions on labor, digital trade, and automotive rules of origin. It was Peña Nieto's most significant diplomatic achievement of his final year.

events.title: Emilio Lozoya Arrested in Spain, Extradited to Mexico
events.detail: Former Pemex CEO Emilio Lozoya Austin — key figure in the Odebrecht bribery scandal — was arrested in Málaga, Spain in February 2020 and extradited to Mexico. Upon return, Lozoya entered a cooperation agreement with the FGR, agreeing to testify against Peña Nieto, former Finance Secretary Luis Videgaray, and senators who allegedly received bribes to pass the energy reform. His testimony represented the most direct criminal exposure Peña Nieto had faced since leaving office.

media.caption: Reuters: Lozoya extradited to Mexico to face corruption charges

events.title: AMLO Truth Commission Declares Ayotzinapa a 'State Crime'; General Arrested
events.detail: AMLO's Subsecretaría de Derechos Humanos released its investigation findings declaring the Ayotzinapa disappearances a 'State crime' — meaning military and federal security agencies bore direct responsibility, not merely criminal cartel actors. General José Rodríguez Pérez, former commander of the 27th Infantry Battalion in Iguala, was arrested in August 2022 on charges of forced disappearance and organized crime. The development validated the GIEI's 2015–2016 findings that had been systematically denied by the Peña Nieto government under the 'verdad histórica.'

media.caption: Proceso: General Rodríguez Pérez arrested for Ayotzinapa disappearances

events.title: SCJN Definitively Acquits Rosario Robles in Estafa Maestra Case
events.detail: Mexico's Supreme Court of Justice (SCJN) ratified the full acquittal of former SEDESOL and SEDATU Secretary Rosario Robles Berlanga in the Estafa Maestra case, closing the criminal investigation against her. Robles had spent nearly three years in preventive detention (2019–2022) accused of 'ejercicio indebido del servicio público' in connection with the MXN $7.67 billion scheme. Her lawyers argued the prosecution was politically motivated by the AMLO government. The acquittal shifted accountability focus toward Emilio Zebadúa González — identified as the actual scheme architect — who subsequently faced administrative proceedings in 2025.

media.caption: Animal Político: SCJN acquits Rosario Robles in Estafa Maestra case

events.title: FGR Opens Pegasus Bribery Investigation: $25M Allegedly Paid to Peña Nieto
events.detail: Mexico's FGR opened a formal investigation into allegations that Israeli businessmen Avishai Neriah and Uri Ansbacher paid approximately $25 million in bribes to Peña Nieto to secure contracts for NSO Group's Pegasus surveillance spyware and military equipment for Mexican security agencies. The FGR requested information from Israeli authorities. The allegations — adding to the still-pending Odebrecht bribery proceedings — deepened the criminal exposure facing the former president, who had been living in Spain since leaving office in November 2018 and publicly denied all allegations.

media.caption: Proceso: FGR investigation opened on alleged Pegasus-related bribes

events.title: CNDH Rejects Ayotzinapa Truth Commission; New Investigation Lines at Iguala Funeral Homes
events.detail: Mexico's CNDH rejected a court order to create a new special truth commission for Ayotzinapa, calling it harmful to ongoing investigations. Meanwhile, the FGR's special unit (UEILCA) opened new investigative lines securing funeral homes and the Semefo forensic services in Iguala, Guerrero, to investigate whether remains were disposed of through these establishments. The parallel developments came more than 11 years after the September 2014 disappearances, as six parents of the 43 students had died without answers, and Tomás Zerón — the official who designed the cover-up — remained in Israel beyond Mexico's extradition reach.

media.caption: Infobae: CNDH rejects Ayotzinapa truth commission as new investigation lines open
